audits.excerpt: "The plague always tasted foul before it landed. Rot, grave-damp, spoiled grain. This hits in clean pulses between breaths. Same hitch. Same answer. Over and over."
audits.reason: The first half finally has carrier and resistance, but the line still resolves into diagnostic shorthand. "Same answer" and "Over and over" flatten the sensation back into report-language instead of keeping it in teeth, breath, and the repeated bodily catch.
audits.suggestedFix: Keep the foul taste and pulse timing, then cut the abstraction: let Jaina say it keeps hitting between breaths with the same hitch each time, using her mouth or lungs as the proof instead of naming an "answer."

audits.excerpt: "The line bends there. We're not following a broad spread anymore. We're closing on one source."
audits.reason: The spoor kink and the snapped chin help, but the spoken line is still map-room language. "Broad spread" and "one source" read like analysis labels instead of something the terrain is forcing on the body in that exact moment.
audits.suggestedFix: Keep her facing the bluffs, then compress the dialogue into the ground itself: the track kinks there, the pull stops smearing across the flats, and everything starts dragging toward one place.

audits.excerpt: "Even under snow, the build was plainly dwarven. The palisade was plain, built for use. Timber trunks stood shoulder to shoulder, banded in iron where humans would have trusted rope."
audits.reason: This drifts off the sourced camp and into a generic frontier stockade. The location material gives Muradin an ice-block-and-packed-snow fort with a levered gate and forge-centered survival logic; swapping that out for timber palisade language weakens both source fidelity and local material identity.
audits.suggestedFix: Replace the stockade description with the rough ice-block circle, the ugly levered gate, and the forge-driven heat economy already established in the Northrend source file so the camp reads like Muradin's camp rather than any cold-weather fort.

audits.excerpt: "As sure as I can be without touching it, and I am not touching it." Her fingers resumed their blind notation, tapping the wet trail she had made in the spilled ale. "I followed it from the coast because it keeps taking the same turn under the noise."
audits.reason: The spilled-ale business grounds the setup, but the payoff still lands in opaque systems-talk. "The same turn under the noise" is too private a diagnostic phrase to carry the scene; the reader needs the repeat translated into something Muradin and the room could cash immediately.
audits.suggestedFix: Keep her finger in the spilled ale, but have her name the repeat in shorter, harder terms—each time the pull comes through the plague-noise, it hooks the same way—so the line stays bodily and audible rather than schematic.

audits.excerpt: "To the right of the mouth, old warning runes had been cut into blue ice and half-scoured by drift. Muradin dropped to one knee long enough to rake a glove across one groove and bare a sharper line beneath the frost."
audits.reason: The runes are present, but they still function as scenery instead of threshold information. In the source material the inscription matters because it states the cost; here the party meets a warning surface and never gets even one actionable fragment of what it warns about.
audits.suggestedFix: Let Muradin clear enough frost for one phrase or cost-marker to register before they move—just enough to make the warning function as a real threshold and not decorative ice-carving.

audits.excerpt: "I heard the seam in it," she said, rubbing numb fingers together once before they flew apart to work again. "A seam. The blade catches a spell there and throws it the same way every time."
audits.reason: The numb fingers help, but the explanation still lands as tidy post-fight terminology. Because this seam becomes future-critical, its first articulation needs to feel discovered through the repeated jerk she is physically tracing, not summarized as a finished diagnostic sentence.
audits.suggestedFix: Keep the numb-finger carrier, then make the line describe the hitch she keeps finding—where the spell gets grabbed and kicked sideways—before or instead of the neat "same way every time" phrasing.
